segments.caption: The video opens with a man in a blue and brown striped shirt, wearing glasses and a goatee, seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork and shelves holding books and candles. The scene transitions to a hand-drawn animation depicting a nighttime riverbank where a figure in a red coat is illuminated by a flashlight beam from two silhouetted officers. The figure raises their hands in surrender, and a speech bubble appears with the words 'LEAVE ME ALONE! I LIVE.' The officers escort the figure toward a police car, and the scene shifts to a mugshot-style illustration of the same person holding a sign reading 'DEC 1945 FRANK POLICE DEPT TACOMA WASH.' The video then cuts back to the man speaking, followed by a title card for 'Crash Course Native American History.' The animation resumes, showing the figure in the red coat kneeling by the river, pulling a fishing net from the water. A historical black-and-white photograph appears, captioned 'Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River Washington (1973),' showing a man holding a large fish. The video returns to the speaker, then to an animation of the man in the red coat flanked by two police officers, followed by a close-up of his face. The final animation shows a group of diverse individuals holding signs that read 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES,' with one person raising a fist in solidarity.

discovered_new_entities.Man in striped shirt: A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, seated in a room with framed artwork and shelves in the background.
discovered_new_entities.Figure in red coat: A hand-drawn character with dark hair, wearing a red coat with a brown collar, depicted in various scenes including being illuminated by a flashlight, surrendering, being escorted by officers, fishing, and standing with a group of protesters.
discovered_new_entities.Police officers: Silhouetted figures in dark uniforms with hats, later shown in blue uniforms with badges, appearing in the animation to confront and escort the figure in the red coat.
A black-and-white photo of a man holding a large fish, captioned 'Billy Frank Jr. fishing on the Nisqually River Washington (1973)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
discovered_new_entities.Protest group: A diverse group of animated individuals holding signs reading 'THIS LAND IS OUR LAND' and 'HONOR THE TREATIES', with one raising a fist, standing in solidarity.
discovered_new_entities.Crash Course Native American History tit: A green textured background with a diamond-shaped logo containing a stylized cup and feathers, with the text 'CRASH COURSE NATIVE AMERICAN HISTORY' below.

segments.caption: The video opens with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs and shelves of books and jars, speaking directly to the camera. He gestures with a rolled paper, then the scene transitions to a split-screen showing historical black-and-white photographs: on the left, a Nisqually fisherman Johnny Bobb holding a fish, and on the right, Nisqually Tribal Member Lou Ann Squally handling a fish in shallow water. The next frame shows arrests during the Fish Wars in Washington (1968–1970), with law enforcement officers detaining individuals. The video returns to the Man in striped shirt, who continues speaking, now with a split-screen graphic defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves,' followed by 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial.' The scene shifts to a close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with traditional Indigenous foods, including colorful corn and herbs, then to a display of dried corn cobs on a wooden surface. The video then shows Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The final frame is an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants with flowers and fruit under a red sun.

segments.caption: The video begins with an illustrated calendar page for June, depicting strawberry plants under a red sun on lined paper, transitioning to a September scene with pawpaw fruit and trees under an orange sun. The scene shifts to the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves in the background, holding a rolled paper. Historical black-and-white photographs appear, showing acorn storage and processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California (c. 1923), including baskets and hands grinding acorns. The Man in striped shirt reappears, followed by an illustrated diagram of 'The Three Sisters'—corn, beans, and squash—growing together on graph paper. The video then presents a map titled 'Native Land Dispossession,' showing pre-colonization Native land in orange, which transitions to a 'Present Day' map with 99% reduction, highlighting land lost to the U.S. in dark green, with only fragmented orange areas remaining. The Man in striped shirt returns, emphasizing the narrative of land loss and cultural practices.

segments.caption: The video begins with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed historical photographs, Native art, and shelves holding books and candles. He speaks directly to the camera, hands clasped, as the scene transitions to a series of historical black-and-white photographs. The first shows students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School in Pennsylvania (1901), seated at long tables in formal attire. This is followed by a photo of the kitchen at Carlisle Indian School (c. 1895), where three women in aprons stand behind a table laden with food. Next, students are shown working in a garden at Carlisle Indian School (c. 1910), tilling the soil with tools under the supervision of an adult. The video returns to the Man in striped shirt, who continues speaking, now with a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign on the wall behind him. The scene shifts to a hand-drawn animation on lined paper, depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees and other figures in traditional attire in the background. This transitions to a historical photograph of a buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming, followed by the completion of the first transcontinental railroad in Utah (1869), showing a large group of men gathered around two trains with a handshake at the center. Throughout, the visuals alternate between the speaker and historical imagery, creating a narrative about Native American history, education, and displacement.

segments.caption: The video begins with a historical photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, emphasizing the scale of bison extermination. The scene transitions to the Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. A quote from a U.S. Army soldier in 1867 appears on screen: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone,' highlighting the deliberate targeting of bison to undermine Native American livelihoods. This is followed by a sepia-toned 1883 illustration titled 'The End' by Martin S. Garretson, depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the aftermath of the bison’s near-extinction. The Man in striped shirt reappears, then a map of Washington State is shown, marking the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, illustrating land loss. A close-up of the 1854 Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc. follows, with Article 3 highlighted: 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' underscoring treaty-protected fishing rights. The segment concludes with a 1905 photograph of men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, symbolizing the exploitation of natural resources, with the Man in striped shirt briefly reappearing to continue his narration.

segments.caption: The video begins with a series of historical black-and-white photographs depicting early 20th-century salmon fishing and canning operations in Washington. The first image shows cannery workers at Apex Fish Co. in 1914, with two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, emphasizing industrial food processing. This transitions to a 1902 photograph of fifty tons of salmon caught in Puget Sound, showcasing a massive pile of fish, highlighting the scale of commercial fishing. Next, a 1906 image shows commercial fishermen unloading salmon from a net onto a dock, illustrating the labor-intensive process of harvesting. The sequence then shifts to the 1968–1970 period, with a colorized image of law enforcement patrolling riverbanks, followed by Native fishers practicing their treaty rights, including a close-up of a fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, symbolizing resistance. The narrative then moves to a modern setting, where the Man in striped shirt speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves, and a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, providing context and commentary. Historical images of Billy Frank Jr. at a congressional hearing in Washington D.C. and Muckleshoot fishers in the Green River in 1970 are shown, underscoring the legal and cultural struggle for fishing rights. The segment concludes with the Man in striped shirt continuing his narration, linking past struggles to present-day sovereignty and food rights.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a series of black-and-white historical photographs depicting key moments in the Native American fishing rights movement in Washington during the 1960s and 1970s. The first image shows Billy Frank Jr. and his family being confronted by law enforcement in 1970, with a man in a long coat pointing authoritatively while others stand nearby in a wooded area. This is followed by a photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in, emphasizing the protest’s public nature. Next, law enforcement vehicles and officers are shown gathering to arrest protestors, highlighting the tension and conflict. A photograph of actor Marlon Brando and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum fishing on the Puyallup River in 1964 underscores the cultural and political significance of the issue. The segment then transitions to a modern-day interview with the Man in striped shirt, who speaks directly to the camera in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, a 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign, and shelves with books and candles. He gestures expressively as he discusses the movement, and a graphic appears beside him explaining 'The Boldt Decision'—the 1974 court ruling that upheld treaty rights and granted Native nations half the harvestable fish catch. A quote from Billy Frank Jr. is displayed: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy,' reinforcing the grassroots, confrontational nature of the struggle. The Man in striped shirt continues speaking, crossing his arms and maintaining a serious demeanor, as the segment concludes with a focus on the legal and personal dimensions of the fight for treaty rights.

discovered_new_entities.The Boldt Decision graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders.

segments.caption: The video begins with the Man in striped shirt seated in a warmly lit room adorned with framed artwork, shelves holding books and candles, and a visible 'YOU ARE ON NATIVE LAND' sign. He speaks directly to the camera with his arms crossed, then gestures with a rolled paper as he explains a topic. The scene transitions to a close-up of golden-brown frybread on a blue and white patterned plate, labeled 'Frybread,' followed by a second image of frybread topped with lettuce, tomatoes, and cheese, served outdoors with a red drink. The narrative shifts to historical black-and-white photographs: first, Navajo people on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), then a map illustrating the forced relocation from Navajo Homelands to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp. Another historical photo shows corn fields at the same reservation. The video then cuts to a modern kitchen scene in Wyoming (2019), where ingredients for frybread—flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, and baking powder—are displayed on a counter. A woman begins mixing the ingredients in a blue bowl, with the text 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo visible. The sequence connects the historical context of displacement and survival with the contemporary practice of making frybread as a cultural and culinary act of resilience.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands mixing ingredients in a blue bowl, labeled 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)', followed by a shot of golden-brown frybread being lifted from a hot oil pan. The scene transitions to the Man in striped shirt, seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. The video then cuts to a series of still images framed with colorful borders: a 'Navajo Taco' stand with a customer ordering, a crowded 'Dine Cafe' with people eating outdoors, and a person harvesting huckleberries in Washington (2022), with a close-up of the berries in a woven basket. Next, a shot of smoked salmon hanging in a smokehouse appears, labeled 'Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Washington (2023)', followed by bags of seeds from the 'Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Oklahoma (2016)'. The final scenes show a woman in a blue jacket standing in a field at the 'Three Sisters Project at Iowa State University Iowa', with close-ups of corn, beans, and squash growing together in the soil, emphasizing traditional agricultural practices.

segments.caption: The video opens with a close-up of a person's ear and hair, identified by on-screen text as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' suggesting a focus on Indigenous culinary expertise. The scene transitions to a group of Native American chefs in white chef uniforms preparing Indigenous cuisine for the White House Tribal Nations Summit in Washington, D.C. (2023), with a woman in a blazer standing beside them. The narrative then shifts to a cooking demonstration titled 'Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone,' showing the preparation of pemmican: blending super dry meat into powder, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying. A man with glasses, a goatee, and black ear piercings, wearing a blue and brown striped shirt, appears seated in a warmly lit room with framed artwork and shelves, speaking directly to the camera. The video then cuts to a buffet of traditional Indigenous foods in Alaska (2016), including a display of dried fish and a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens. This is followed by a scene of Suquamish salmon preparation in Washington (2018), with salmon being smoked over a fire. The segment concludes with an aerial view of a dam on the Nisqually River, emphasizing the environmental and cultural significance of waterways to Indigenous communities.

entity_library.Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to govern themselves', set against a green textured background with colorful borders.
entity_library.Food Sovereignty definition graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'Food Sovereignty' as 'The right of Native nations to use the foods that have sustained their communities since time immemorial', set against a green textured background with colorful borders.
entity_library.Traditional Indigenous foods basket: A close-up of hands holding a woven basket filled with colorful corn, herbs, and other traditional Indigenous foods, with a turquoise beaded necklace visible on the person’s chest.
entity_library.Dried corn cobs display: A wooden surface displaying rows of dried corn cobs with multicolored kernels, alongside jars of beans and other grains.
entity_library.Suquamish salmon preparation footage: A video clip showing salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, captioned 'Suquamish salmon preparation Washington (2018)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.June calendar illustration: An illustrated calendar page for June, showing strawberry plants with green leaves, white flowers, and red strawberries, under a red sun and a blue sky with stars, drawn on lined paper.
entity_library.Historical photograph of acorn storage: A black-and-white photograph of two individuals standing beside a large woven acorn storage basket in California (c. 1923), framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of baskets used in: A black-and-white photograph showing baskets and acorns used in the preparation of acorn flour in California (c. 1923), framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Acorn processing methods of the Southwes: A close-up video clip showing hands grinding acorns in a bowl, with text overlay describing the traditional processing methods of the Southwestern Pomo Indians of California.
entity_library.The Three Sisters illustration: An illustrated diagram on graph paper showing corn, beans, and squash growing together, labeled 'The Three Sisters,' with corn as the central stalk, beans climbing it, and squash spreading at the base.
entity_library.Native Land Dispossession map: A series of maps showing the United States, first labeled 'Pre-colonization' with orange representing Native land, then transitioning to 'Present Day' with dark green indicating land lost to the U.S. and fragmented orange areas for remaining Native land, with a '99% Reduction' label.
entity_library.Historical photograph of students in the: A black-and-white photo showing numerous students in formal attire seated at long tables in a large dining hall, captioned 'Students in the dining hall at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (1901)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of the kitchen at: A black-and-white photo showing three women in aprons standing behind a long table filled with food, captioned 'The kitchen at Carlisle Indian School, Pennsylvania (c. 1895)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Carlisle Indian: A black-and-white photo showing students tilling soil with tools in a garden, with a building and an adult supervisor in the background, captioned 'Carlisle Indian School students working in a garden, Pennsylvania (c. 1910)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Hand-drawn animation on lined paper: A colorful, hand-drawn illustration on lined paper depicting a Native figure on a blue horse hunting buffalo, with teepees, other figures in traditional attire, and animals in the background, rendered in a childlike, stylized manner.
entity_library.Historical photograph of buffalo herd in: A black-and-white photograph showing a herd of buffalo grazing in a wide, open valley with hills in the background, captioned 'Buffalo herd in the Lamar Valley in Wyoming', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of completion of t: A black-and-white photograph showing a large group of men gathered around two trains, with two men shaking hands at the center, captioned 'Completion of the first transcontinental railroad in the U.S., Utah (1869)', framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of bison skulls: A sepia-toned photograph from 1892 showing a massive pile of bison skulls in Michigan, with men standing at its base and one atop the mound, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of men with spruce: A sepia-toned photograph from 1905 showing men posing with a massive spruce log in the Cascade Mountains, Washington, with horses and a log cabin in the background, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical illustration 'The End' by Mar: A sepia-toned 1883 illustration depicting a desolate landscape littered with bison bones, a wagon, and a lone figure, symbolizing the end of the bison era, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Map of Nisqually territory: A green and yellow map of Washington State showing the Nisqually Reservation and the approximate ceded Nisqually territory, with labeled regions and a red triangle marking the reservation, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Treaty with the Nisqually, Puyallup, etc: A close-up of the 1854 treaty text, with Article 3 highlighted in orange, stating 'The right of taking fish, at all usual and accustomed grounds and stations, is further secured to said Indians,' framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Quote from U.S. Army soldier (1867): A text graphic displaying the quote: 'Kill every buffalo you can. Every buffalo dead is an Indian gone.' attributed to a U.S. Army soldier in 1867, set against a textured background with colorful borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Muckleshoot pra: A black-and-white photograph from 1970 showing several men wading in the Green River, using long poles to fish, with cars and onlookers visible on the riverbank, illustrating treaty fishing rights in practice.
entity_library.Historical photograph of commercial fish: A black-and-white image from 1906 showing two fishermen on a dock, pulling a large net filled with salmon, with a pile of fish already unloaded on the dock, emphasizing commercial fishing labor.
entity_library.Historical photograph of cannery workers: A black-and-white photograph from 1914 showing two women in aprons and hairnets standing beside a long table filled with rows of canned fish, illustrating industrial food processing.
entity_library.Historical photograph of fifty tons of s: A black-and-white photograph from 1902 showing a massive pile of salmon, emphasizing the scale of commercial fishing in Puget Sound.
entity_library.Native fishers practicing their treaty f: A colorized photograph from 1968–1970 showing a Native fisher in a boat with a 'FISH FIGHT' sign, casting a net, symbolizing resistance and the assertion of treaty rights.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Al Bridges: A black-and-white photo of activist Al Bridges speaking into a bullhorn at a fish-in in Washington (1970), wearing a headband and traditional attire, standing on a vehicle with trees in the background.
entity_library.Historical photograph of law enforcement: A black-and-white photo showing police vehicles and officers gathering to arrest protestors in Washington (1970), with 'POLICE' written on the side of a car and officers in uniform standing near trees.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Marlon Brando a: A black-and-white photo of actor Marlon Brando (left) and Puyallup activist Bob Satiacum holding fish on the Puyallup River in Washington (1964), with Brando smiling and Satiacum holding a fish with a net.
entity_library.The Boldt Decision graphic: A split-screen graphic with the Man in striped shirt on the left and a text box on the right defining 'The Boldt Decision' as the 1974 court ruling that upheld Treaty Rights and ruled that Native nations in Washington were entitled to half of the harvestable catch of fish, set against a dark textured background with colorful borders.
entity_library.Billy Frank Jr. quote graphic: A graphic displaying the quote: '[I] was not a policy guy... I was a getting-arrested guy.' attributed to Billy Frank Jr., shown alongside a portrait of him in a white shirt with a turquoise necklace, framed with colorful borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Navajo on the B: A black-and-white photograph showing Navajo people gathered around horses and buildings on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Map of Navajo Homelands and Bosque Redon: A green-toned map showing the Navajo Homelands in orange, with a red wavy line indicating the forced relocation to the Bosque Redondo Reservation and Internment Camp in New Mexico, framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Historical photograph of Corn Fields - B: A black-and-white photograph depicting Navajo people working in corn fields on the Bosque Redondo Indian Reservation in New Mexico (c. 1863–1868), framed with colorful geometric borders.
entity_library.Frybread: Golden-brown, puffy fried bread shown being lifted from a hot oil pan, with a close-up of its crispy texture and golden surface.
entity_library.Wyoming PBS footage of making frybread: A modern kitchen scene showing ingredients for frybread (flour, nonfat dry milk, salt, baking powder) on a wooden counter, with a woman mixing them in a blue bowl. Text overlay reads 'Making frybread on the Wind River Indian Reservation Wyoming (2019)' and the Wyoming PBS logo is visible.
entity_library.Frybread / Navajo Taco stand: A small white and blue food stand with a large 'NAVAJO TACO' sign, offering 'HOT FRESH FRY BREAD $5.00', with a customer placing an order at the window.
entity_library.Muckleshoot huckleberry harvest Washingt: A person in a red shirt and woven hat harvesting huckleberries in a forest, with a close-up of the dark purple and red berries in a woven basket.
entity_library.Cherokee traditional salmon smoking Wash: Smoked salmon strips hanging in a smokehouse, with a text overlay indicating the location and year.
entity_library.Cherokee Nation Heirloom Seed Program Ok: Clear plastic bags labeled 'Cherokee White Eagle Corn' and other heirloom seeds, with the Cherokee Nation Administration Support Dept. address printed on the bags.
entity_library.Three Sisters Project at Iowa State Univ: A woman in a blue jacket and black pants standing in a field observing rows of corn, beans, and squash growing together, with close-ups of the plants in the soil.
entity_library.Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef': A close-up of a person's ear and hair, with on-screen text identifying them as Shawn Sherman, 'The Sioux Chef,' indicating their role as a prominent Indigenous culinary figure.
entity_library.Native American chefs preparing Indigeno: A group of chefs in white uniforms, including one wearing a face mask, standing behind a food preparation counter with ingredients and tools, alongside a woman in a blazer, in a modern kitchen setting.
entity_library.Indigikitchen with Mariah Gladstone: A cooking demonstration showing the steps to make pemmican: blending dry meat, adding wild rice, pressing into patties, and frying, with on-screen text guiding each step.
entity_library.Traditional Indigenous foods Alaska (201: A buffet scene with hands serving food, including a plate with a bowl of soup and a side of greens, and a large tray of dried fish, set in a rustic wooden interior.
entity_library.Suquamish salmon preparation Washington: Salmon being smoked over a fire using a traditional wooden rack, with flames visible beneath the fish, emphasizing traditional food preparation methods.
entity_library.Dam on the Nisqually River: An aerial view of a large concrete dam spanning the Nisqually River, surrounded by green trees and a road, highlighting the infrastructure's impact on Indigenous waterways.
